question:
2. \( A_{1} A_{2} \ldots A_{n} \) is a regular polygon inscribed in a circle of radius \( r \) and center \( O . P \) is a point on line \( O A_{1} \) extended beyond \( A_{1} \). Show that
\[
\prod_{i=1}^{n} \overline{P A}_{i}=\overline{O P}^{n}-r^{n}
\]
solution:
Solution. We may assume the polygon is in the complex plane with its center at the origin and \( A_{1} \) on the positive real axis. Then the other vertices are
\[
r \omega, r \omega^{2}, \ldots, r \omega^{n-1}
\]
where \( \omega \) is a primitive \( n \)th root of unity.
If \( P \) is at the point \( x \), then \( \overline{P A_{i}}=\left|x-r \omega^{i-1}\right| \). So
\[
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{i=1}^{n} \overline{P A_{i}} & =\left|\prod_{i=1}^{n}\left(x-r \omega^{i-1}\right)\right|=r^{n}\left|\prod_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{x}{r}-\omega^{i-1}\right)\right|=r^{n}\left|\left(\frac{x}{r}\right)^{n}-1\right| \\
& =\left|x^{n}-r^{n}\right|=x^{n}-r^{n}=\overline{O P}^{n}-r^{n} .
\end{aligned}
\]

At the third step we used the factorization
\[
X^{n}-1=\prod_{i=1}^{n}\left(X-\omega^{i-1}\right)
\]
which is valid because \( 1, \omega, \omega^{2}, \ldots, \omega^{n-1} \) are the zeros of \( X^{n}-1 \).

descriptive_long.question:
2. \( vertexon vertextw \ldots vertexnn \) is a regular polygon inscribed in a circle of radius \( radiusln \) and center \( centerpt . pointpe \) is a point on line \( centerpt vertexon \) extended beyond \( vertexon \). Show that
\[
\prod_{indexvar=1}^{sidesnum} \overline{pointpe vertexii}=\overline{centerpt pointpe}^{sidesnum}-radiusln^{sidesnum}
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We may assume the polygon is in the complex plane with its center at the origin and \( vertexon \) on the positive real axis. Then the other vertices are
\[
radiusln\ rootunit, radiusln\ rootunit^{2}, \ldots, radiusln\ rootunit^{sidesnum-1}
\]
where \( rootunit \) is a primitive \( sidesnum \)th root of unity.
If \( pointpe \) is at the point \( complexx \), then \( \overline{pointpe\ vertexii}=\left|complexx-radiusln\ rootunit^{indexvar-1}\right| \). So
\[
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{indexvar=1}^{sidesnum} \overline{pointpe\ vertexii} & =\left|\prod_{indexvar=1}^{sidesnum}\left(complexx-radiusln\ rootunit^{indexvar-1}\right)\right|=radiusln^{sidesnum}\left|\prod_{indexvar=1}^{sidesnum}\left(\frac{complexx}{radiusln}-rootunit^{indexvar-1}\right)\right|=radiusln^{sidesnum}\left|\left(\frac{complexx}{radiusln}\right)^{sidesnum}-1\right| \\
& =\left|complexx^{sidesnum}-radiusln^{sidesnum}\right|=complexx^{sidesnum}-radiusln^{sidesnum}=\overline{centerpt\ pointpe}^{sidesnum}-radiusln^{sidesnum} .
\end{aligned}
\]

At the third step we used the factorization
\[
symbolex^{sidesnum}-1=\prod_{indexvar=1}^{sidesnum}\left(symbolex-rootunit^{indexvar-1}\right)
\]
which is valid because \( 1, rootunit, rootunit^{2}, \ldots, rootunit^{sidesnum-1} \) are the zeros of \( symbolex^{sidesnum}-1 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
2. \( jellyfish snowflake \ldots gemstone \) is a regular polygon inscribed in a circle of radius \( stargazer \) and center \( shipwreck . butterfly \) is a point on line \( shipwreck jellyfish \) extended beyond \( jellyfish \). Show that
\[
\prod_{seashell=1}^{pinecones} \overline{butterfly driftwood}=\overline{shipwreck butterfly}^{pinecones}-stargazer^{pinecones}
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. We may assume the polygon is in the complex plane with its center at the origin and \( jellyfish \) on the positive real axis. Then the other vertices are
\[
stargazer raincloud, stargazer raincloud^{2}, \ldots, stargazer raincloud^{pinecones-1}
\]
where \( raincloud \) is a primitive \( pinecones \)th root of unity.
If \( butterfly \) is at the point \( rainwater \), then \( \overline{butterfly driftwood}=\left|rainwater-stargazer raincloud^{seashell-1}\right| \). So
\[
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{seashell=1}^{pinecones} \overline{butterfly driftwood} & =\left|\prod_{seashell=1}^{pinecones}\left(rainwater-stargazer raincloud^{seashell-1}\right)\right|=stargazer^{pinecones}\left|\prod_{seashell=1}^{pinecones}\left(\frac{rainwater}{stargazer}-raincloud^{seashell-1}\right)\right|=stargazer^{pinecones}\left|\left(\frac{rainwater}{stargazer}\right)^{pinecones}-1\right| \\
& =\left|rainwater^{pinecones}-stargazer^{pinecones}\right|=rainwater^{pinecones}-stargazer^{pinecones}=\overline{shipwreck butterfly}^{pinecones}-stargazer^{pinecones} .
\end{aligned}
\]

At the third step we used the factorization
\[
lighthouse^{pinecones}-1=\prod_{seashell=1}^{pinecones}\left(lighthouse-raincloud^{seashell-1}\right)
\]
which is valid because \( 1, raincloud, raincloud^{2}, \ldots, raincloud^{pinecones-1} \) are the zeros of \( lighthouse^{pinecones}-1 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
2. \( lastvertex middlevertex \ldots absentvertex \) is a regular polygon inscribed in a circle of radius \( diameterlength \) and center \( edgepoint . insidepoint \) is a point on line \( edgepoint lastvertex \) extended beyond \( lastvertex \). Show that
\[
\prod_{aggregate=1}^{singularity} \overline{insidepoint sidepoint}= \overline{edgepoint insidepoint}^{singularity}-diameterlength^{singularity}
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. We may assume the polygon is in the complex plane with its center at the origin and \( lastvertex \) on the positive real axis. Then the other vertices are
\[
diameterlength\, realnumber,\; diameterlength\, realnumber^{2}, \ldots, diameterlength\, realnumber^{singularity-1}
\]
where \( realnumber \) is a primitive \( singularity \)th root of unity.
If \( insidepoint \) is at the point \( imaginaryvalue \), then \( \overline{insidepoint sidepoint}=\left|imaginaryvalue-diameterlength\, realnumber^{aggregate-1}\right| \). So
\[
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{aggregate=1}^{singularity} \overline{insidepoint sidepoint} & =\left|\prod_{aggregate=1}^{singularity}\left(imaginaryvalue-diameterlength\, realnumber^{aggregate-1}\right)\right|=diameterlength^{singularity}\left|\prod_{aggregate=1}^{singularity}\left(\frac{imaginaryvalue}{diameterlength}-realnumber^{aggregate-1}\right)\right| \\
& =diameterlength^{singularity}\left|\left(\frac{imaginaryvalue}{diameterlength}\right)^{singularity}-1\right| \\
& =\left|imaginaryvalue^{singularity}-diameterlength^{singularity}\right|=imaginaryvalue^{singularity}-diameterlength^{singularity}=\overline{edgepoint insidepoint}^{singularity}-diameterlength^{singularity} .
\end{aligned}
\]

At the third step we used the factorization
\[
constantvalue^{singularity}-1=\prod_{aggregate=1}^{singularity}\left(constantvalue-realnumber^{aggregate-1}\right)
\]
which is valid because \( 1, realnumber, realnumber^{2}, \ldots, realnumber^{singularity-1} \) are the zeros of \( constantvalue^{singularity}-1 \).

garbled_string.question:
2. \( asdfghjk qweruiop \ldots zxcvlkjh \) is a regular polygon inscribed in a circle of radius \( zmxncbva \) and center \( plokmijn . qzxwvtnp \) is a point on line \( plokmijn asdfghjk \) extended beyond \( asdfghjk \). Show that
\[
\prod_{zasdghjk=1}^{lqwertyu} \overline{qzxwvtnp A}_{zasdghjk}=\overline{plokmijn qzxwvtnp}^{lqwertyu}-zmxncbva^{lqwertyu}
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. We may assume the polygon is in the complex plane with its center at the origin and \( asdfghjk \) on the positive real axis. Then the other vertices are
\[
zmxncbva kjhgfdsa, zmxncbva kjhgfdsa^{2}, \ldots, zmxncbva kjhgfdsa^{lqwertyu-1}
\]
where \( kjhgfdsa \) is a primitive \( lqwertyu \)th root of unity.
If \( qzxwvtnp \) is at the point \( hjgrksla \), then \( \overline{qzxwvtnp poiulkjh}=\left|hjgrksla-zmxncbva kjhgfdsa^{zasdghjk-1}\right| \). So
\[
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{zasdghjk=1}^{lqwertyu} \overline{qzxwvtnp poiulkjh} & =\left|\prod_{zasdghjk=1}^{lqwertyu}\left(hjgrksla-zmxncbva kjhgfdsa^{zasdghjk-1}\right)\right|=zmxncbva^{lqwertyu}\left|\prod_{zasdghjk=1}^{lqwertyu}\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{zmxncbva}-kjhgfdsa^{zasdghjk-1}\right)\right|=zmxncbva^{lqwertyu}\left|\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{zmxncbva}\right)^{lqwertyu}-1\right| \\
& =\left|hjgrksla^{lqwertyu}-zmxncbva^{lqwertyu}\right|=hjgrksla^{lqwertyu}-zmxncbva^{lqwertyu}=\overline{plokmijn qzxwvtnp}^{lqwertyu}-zmxncbva^{lqwertyu} .
\end{aligned}
\]

At the third step we used the factorization
\[
mldkfjwe^{lqwertyu}-1=\prod_{zasdghjk=1}^{lqwertyu}\left(mldkfjwe-kjhgfdsa^{zasdghjk-1}\right)
\]
which is valid because \( 1, kjhgfdsa, kjhgfdsa^{2}, \ldots, kjhgfdsa^{lqwertyu-1} \) are the zeros of \( mldkfjwe^{lqwertyu}-1 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  3.  Two concentric regular n-gons are inscribed in the same plane circle with center O and radii r < R.  Their vertices are  
A_1,A_2,\ldots ,A_n  (radius r) and B_1,B_2,\ldots ,B_n (radius R),  
with A_1 and B_1 lying on the same ray OA_1.  A point P is chosen on that ray so that r < OP < R.  Prove that  
  \prod _{i=1}^{n}  PA_i  / \prod _{i=1}^{n}  PB_i  = (R^n - OP^n)/(OP^n - r^n).
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  Place O at the origin of the complex plane and put A_1 on the positive real axis.  With \omega  = e^{2\pi i/n},  
 A_k = r \omega ^{k-1}, B_k = R \omega ^{k-1} (1 \leq  k \leq  n).

Step 2.  Let P have coordinate x, where r < x < R (x is real).  Then  
 PA_k = |x - r \omega ^{k-1}|, PB_k = |x - R \omega ^{k-1}|,  
so  
 \prod  PA_k = |\prod (x - r \omega ^{k-1})|, \prod  PB_k = |\prod (x - R \omega ^{k-1})|.   (\star )

Step 3.  Pull r or R out of every factor in (\star ) and use  
 X^n - 1 = \prod (X - \omega ^{k-1}).  
Thus  
 \prod  PA_k = |x^n - r^n|, \prod  PB_k = |x^n - R^n|.   (\dagger )

Step 4.  Because x is real with r < x < R, the quantities in (\dagger ) are positive, so the absolute-value signs may be removed:  
 \prod _{k=1}^{n} PA_k / \prod _{k=1}^{n} PB_k = (x^n - r^n)/(R^n - x^n).  
Finally substitute x = OP to obtain  
 \prod _{i=1}^{n} PA_i / \prod _{i=1}^{n} PB_i = (R^n - OP^n)/(OP^n - r^n),  
as required.

_replacement_note.reason: Original kernel variant was too easy compared to the original problem
